narrative: Your law school application was denied because our predictive model, which assesses whether applicants are likely to pass the bar exam on their first try, estimated a 99.2% chance that you would not pass it. This high predicted risk of failure directly led to the rejection, as the school prioritizes admitting students with strong indicators of bar success to maintain high pass rates. For context, you're a Black male with an upper-middle-class family background, planning to attend full-time, and your undergraduate GPA sits at 2.90—below the typical 3.21 average—while your LSAT score of 34 is also under the average of 36.70. The model's decision hinged most heavily on your Year 3 school decile ranking of 2, which placed you in the bottom tenth of your peers and strongly pushed the prediction toward expecting bar failure, far more than any other factor. Next in influence was your Year 1 decile of 2, similarly low compared to the average around 5 or 6, which further reinforced the model's concern about your early academic performance carrying over to the bar. Your family's upper-middle income level played a smaller but still notable role, nudging the prediction slightly toward failure, possibly because the model associates certain income brackets with other patterns in bar outcomes. These three elements—your deciles in Years 3 and 1, plus family income—stood out as the top drivers, outweighing aspects like your LSAT or GPA in shaping this outcome for you specifically. Compared to typical students who get approved, your decile rankings are notably lower, signaling to the model a tougher path to bar success than most. While full-time status is common and didn't sway things much, the combination here painted a riskier picture overall. This isn't a judgment on your potential or background—it's the model highlighting patterns from past students where similar profiles correlated with bar struggles. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score closer to or above average, strengthening your grades in any ongoing studies to improve your decile standings, and exploring programs with bar prep support tailored for diverse applicants like yourself. You might also appeal with evidence of recent improvements or connect with mentors who’ve succeeded in similar circumstances to build a stronger case for reconsideration elsewhere. Keep pushing; many have turned around predictions like this through targeted effort.
